text: The surgeon examines the indirect hernia sac and internal ring, attempting to separate normal fatty tissue from the hernia sac. A large piece of retroperitoneal fat is visible, and the leading edge of the hernia sac is identified. The surgeon dissects medially past the midline, encountering a torn vessel that leads to pooling of blood in the retropubic space.

Blood is visibly pooling in the retropubic space, increasing the risk of injury to the bladder, obturator vessels, nerve, and coronamortis. The surgeon attempts to control the bleeding using bipolar cautery. The hernia sac has been reduced, revealing a large piece of medial vocal ligament fat and a clump of fat hanging off the epigastric vessel. The testicular vessels are pushed laterally, separated from the fatty tissue contributing to the hernia bulge.

The surgeon cautiously dissects around the vas deferens and testicular vessels, addressing a small vessel bleeding on the medial side. The dissection reveals a distinct plane separating fat from the inferior gastric and lateral retroperitoneal fat. Visible vessels and lymphatics are divided to reduce the fat, with plans to clip lymphatics later to prevent bleeding. Blood pooling is observed in the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects two centimeters deep into the retropubic space to obtain the critical view of the myopectic ulcer. The transfer salus sling is visible. The surgeon strategically moves to the right of midline, where there is no bleeding, to find the correct plane and then proceeds towards the pool of blood, ensuring it remains contained and does not infiltrate the clean area.

The surgeon continues to prioritize the cord structures, identifying a natural tissue plane between the medial molecular fat and the vas deferens. Hemostasis is achieved, revealing the shiny fat adjacent to the bleeding area, which contains the obturator nodes. The vas deferens is positioned away from the dissection plane, allowing the surgeon to peel fat off the vas and testicular vessels. A piece of mesh is prepared to be inserted vertically into the retropubic space, aiming to cover the obturator nodes and vas, preventing fat from slipping underneath.

The surgeon exposes the obturator node and creates a small window to suture to Cooper's ligament. A wide packet of obturator nodes is visible. The medial umbilical ligament area is shown, with a tubular structure that resembles the medial umbilical ligament but is left undisturbed due to anatomical distortion. Lymphatics and vessels around the structure are divided, and a clip is applied. The tubular structure is traced along the medial umbilical ligament fold, lifting it and tethering the fold. The vas deferens is visible, positioned very laterally due to the large medial umbilical ligament path.

The surgeon identifies a large medial umbilical ligament fat displacing the testicular vessels. There is noticeable bleeding, attributed to different energy settings used earlier in the procedure. The surgeon requests a mega suture cut needle driver to prepare for mesh placement, revealing a large defect with a visible lymph node and a previously divided lymphatic channel.

The surgeon uses a Raytech to blot blood near the medial umbilical ligament. An extra-large 3D Max mid left side mesh is visible in the abdomen, placed before docking the robot. The surgeon tucks the mesh to prevent herniation of the fatty packet beneath it, securing the mesh with a varicose suture to Cooper's ligament due to the large defect.

The surgeon places additional sutures around the larger defect to ensure the mesh is securely positioned, preventing it from bulging out. The mesh is carefully secured around the internal ring, with attention to the transverse salus, which may be sewn down to Cooper's ligament for direct defects.

The surgeon pauses to take pictures, showing the reduced size of the fat that was contributing to the palpable mass. The bedside assistant inserts a suction cannula to rapidly desufflate the pocket by allowing intra-abdominal pressure to push CO₂ out.
